feedback.text: I'd like to start off by talking about my worries about this act. Due to the timing of this act, I can't help but compare it to UK's recently released Online Safety Act. I think the Online Safety Act and other similar acts trying to be passed allow for way too much government overreach and censorship. My hope is that this act does not end up the same way. I believe the best way to achieve that would be for the act to not involve anything about the ethics of online content (especially adult content) and stick to deceptiveness in online practices. I believe this approach will aptly protect consumers while preventing government overreach. My second point is about specific consumer protections I'd like to see in the act. I believe that live service video games have adopted anti-consumer practices when it comes to sunsetting games when they're no longer able to keep up relevant servers. I believe this act should include regulations for videogame companies that mandate that live service video games be left in a functional playable state when the company decides to end support. I believe that when most people purchase live service video games, they are under the impression that they're getting a product but the companies being able to revoke access to that product when it's no longer convenient for them runs contrary to that intuition. Considering that the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative, which is trying to address this exact issue, has over 1.4 million signatures, it's clear there are many people who agree with me on this. So, I think this act should label leaving a video game that someone has purchased in an unplayable state as a deceptive practice and implement methods to penalize companies involved in such practices. Thank you for listening to my feedback.
